coredata.dc:title: Visible light-activated NGQD/nsC3N4/Bi2WO6 microsphere composite for effluent organic matter treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Truong, Hai Bang
Huy, Bui The
Ray, Schindra Kumar
Lee, Yong-Ill
Cho, Jinwoo
Hur, Jin
coredata.dc:description: The diminishing availability of clean water resources has led to a high demand for advanced, effective wastewater effluent reclamation techniques. In this study, a new microsphere composite of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots, nanosheet C3N4, and Bi2WO6 was synthesized and characterized to examine its feasibility as a visible light-activated photocatalyst for wastewater reclamation. At the optimal dose of 1 g•L−1 under 6 h of illumination, the composite exhibited a high removal capability for effluent organic matter (82.8 ± 1.6%). Its high performance and stability over multiple cycles are attributed to two factors: (1) its harvesting ability over a wide range of the visible light region and (2) its ability to separate photoinduced charge carriers effectively, which is driven by the efficient interfacial contact of the hosts and inclusion of the photosensitized nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots in the structural network. The complex effluent organic matter removal process of the compound was further examined by fluorescence spectroscopy and size exclusion chromatography. Fluorescence spectroscopy showed that humic-like components with a higher degree of aromatic condensation and greater molecular size were preferentially removed by photocatalysis. Protein-like components were removed more rapidly than the humic-like components due to the synergetic effects of OH and 1O2 on the rapid decomposition of low-molecular-weight compounds. Size exclusion chromatography revealed that the photocatalytic system preferentially removed larger molecules via adsorption and oxidation; however, smaller size fractions might be produced via the breakdown of biopolymer and humic substance fractions in effluent organic matter.

head.ce:title: Visible light-activated NGQD/nsC3N4/Bi2WO6 microsphere composite for effluent organic matter treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The diminishing availability of clean water resources has led to a high demand for advanced, effective wastewater effluent reclamation techniques. In this study, a new microsphere composite of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots, nanosheet C3N4, and Bi2WO6 was synthesized and characterized to examine its feasibility as a visible light-activated photocatalyst for wastewater reclamation. At the optimal dose of 1 g•L−1 under 6 h of illumination, the composite exhibited a high removal capability for effluent organic matter (82.8 ± 1.6%). Its high performance and stability over multiple cycles are attributed to two factors: (1) its harvesting ability over a wide range of the visible light region and (2) its ability to separate photoinduced charge carriers effectively, which is driven by the efficient interfacial contact of the hosts and inclusion of the photosensitized nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots in the structural network. The complex effluent organic matter removal process of the compound was further examined by fluorescence spectroscopy and size exclusion chromatography. Fluorescence spectroscopy showed that humic-like components with a higher degree of aromatic condensation and greater molecular size were preferentially removed by photocatalysis. Protein-like components were removed more rapidly than the humic-like components due to the synergetic effects of OH and 1O2 on the rapid decomposition of low-molecular-weight compounds. Size exclusion chromatography revealed that the photocatalytic system preferentially removed larger molecules via adsorption and oxidation; however, smaller size fractions might be produced via the breakdown of biopolymer and humic substance fractions in effluent organic matter.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Excitation-emission matrix coupled with parallel factor analysis

ce:section.ce:para: The diminishing availability of clean water resources has been accelerated by rapid population and urbanization growth, subsequently increasing water pollution and global warming. Therefore, many studies have been conducted to develop economic and efficient water treatment technologies. In areas with insufficient clean water supplies, the utilization of reclaimed wastewater as a substitute for clean water has been suggested. Effluent organic matter (EfOM), which refers to the dissolved organic matter (DOM) in treated wastewater, contains soluble microbial products, harmful micropollutants, and refractory organic compounds such as humic substances (HS) [1] (abbreviations utilized in this study are listed in Table 1 ). Numerous organic compounds contained in EfOM may hinder reclamation treatment operations by acting as precursors for disinfection byproducts, nutrients for microorganisms, organic foulants in membrane filtration, and consumers of applied oxidants and coagulants [2,3].

To facilitate wastewater reclamation and meet effluent discharge regulations, several advanced techniques have been implemented, such as coagulation/flocculation [4], adsorption [5], disinfection [6], filtration [7], bioremediation [2], and advanced oxidation processes [8,9]. Advanced oxidation processes have garnered significant attention due to their outstanding performance in removing recalcitrant compounds without generating sludge. Among the various advanced oxidation processes, photocatalysis is a highly effective, environmentally friendly treatment [10]. Most studies on the photocatalysis treatment of EfOM or natural organic matter (NOM) have relied on ultraviolet (UV)-activated catalysts such as TiO2 and ZnO [11,12]. Recently, visible light-activated photocatalysts have been investigated as a green technology to exploit the unlimited solar light [10]; however, visible light-activated photocatalysis applications in water treatment for EfOM and NOM removal have not been studied. Furthermore, the complex removal mechanisms arising from the heterogeneous composition of EfOM and NOM are not yet fully understood (see Table S1 for details).

The utilization of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a robust non-metallic semiconductor, as an eco-friendly photocatalyst for water remediation has increased due to its chemical and thermal stability, tunable electronic properties, cost-effectiveness, and narrow bandgap [13]. Despite these advantages, there remain several disadvantages, including the ultra-rapid recombination of exciton-hole pairs and narrow light absorption range below 460 nm, which restrict the photocatalytic potency of pristine g-C3N4 [14]. Various strategies have been employed to overcome these limitations, including the hybridization of g-C3N4 with other materials to produce heterogeneous catalytic systems. For example, Truong et al. [8] prepared a composite of g-C3N4 and Zn/Bi2O3 to remove NOM from water; however, the composite exhibited an unsatisfactory performance for NOM removal (2.6 mgC removed per gram of catalyst) after 5 h of illumination. In another study [9], a synthesized heterostructure of nanosheet carbon nitride (nsC3N4) and WO3 exhibited good performance for NOM removal (13.6 mgC removed per gram of catalyst) with the addition of H2O2 into the photocatalytic system. Bi2WO6 exhibits large ferroelectric spontaneous polarization (50 μC/cm2) and an equivalent narrow-bandgap energy to that of C3N4 (~2.7 eV), which could be employed as a visible-light-driven component [15]. C3N4/Bi2WO6-based heterojunction composites have exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance in removing simple organic pollutants, such as dyes and drugs [16–18]. However, the combination of C3N4 and Bi2WO6 has not been explored for the photocatalytic treatment of EfOM and NOM. Auxiliary materials serving as electron shuttle mediators can be supplemented in small quantities during synthesis to promote charge transmission in multi-component composites [17,18]. Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (NGQDs) are considered the best candidate to achieve this goal due to their significant quantum confinement and electrical conductivity. NGQDs contain nitrogen atoms between the conjugated double bond lattices, which provide a favorable network for electron transfer [19]. Furthermore, NGQDs possess an upconversion photoluminescence (PL) property, which can extend the visible light adsorption region when added to a composite [20].

Understanding the photocatalytic behavior of EfOM removal requires advanced characterization tools to determine the heterogeneous composition of EfOM that contains several organic compounds. Fluorescence excitation-emission matrix (EEM) parallel factor analysis (EEM-PARAFAC) is a chemometric tool for deconvoluting the EEM dataset into independent fluorescent components, which can be beneficial for this purpose [21,22]. Individual EEM-PARAFAC components, representing different DOM origins and structures, have been utilized as surrogates for the DOM processability and treatment efficiency in diverse water treatment processes [23]. Applying EEM-PARAFAC for the photocatalytic decomposition of EfOM can reveal the different removal behaviors of different components within bulk EfOM [24,25]. In addition to spectroscopic techniques that only reflect light-absorbing organic molecules [24], size exclusion chromatography with organic carbon detection (SEC-OCD) enables the separation of different-sized fractions within bulk EfOM and tracking of the individual removal behaviors during photocatalysis [26,27].

This study fabricated and examined the feasibility of utilizing a ternary photocatalytic composite consisting of NGQD, C3N4, and Bi2WO6 for wastewater reclamation. The major objectives of this study were to synthesize the ternary composite (NGQD/C3N4/Bi2WO6), examine its feasibility as a visible light-activated photocatalyst for wastewater reclamation, and acquire a better understanding of the complex removal mechanisms of EfOM using advanced characterization tools such as EEM-PARAFAC and SEC-OCD.

The reagents used in this study were analytical-grade urea, citric acid, Na2WO4·2H2O, Bi(NO3)3.5 H2O, KI, p-benzoquinone, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), furfuryl alcohol, 2,3-Bis(2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide inner salt (XTT), and singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG), which were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Thermo Fisher Scientific. For the EfOM sample, clarified secondary effluent was collected from a local municipal wastewater treatment facility (Seoul, Korea) in 2020. The EfOM sample was filtered through a cellulose acetate membrane filter (0.45 μm pore size, Whatman®) and stored at 4 °C until use in the experiments. The initial EfOM sample had a dissolved organic carbon (DOC) concentration of 8.3 ± 0.3 mgC•L−1 and a pH of 6.7 ± 0.1. For comparison, surface water DOM samples were collected at a depth of 1 m from the Han River (Seoul, South Korea), which serves as a major drinking water source to the Seoul Metropolitan Area in South Korea. The surface water samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm cellulose acetate filter and concentrated utilizing a rotary evaporator to obtain a DOC concentration similar to that of the EfOM sample.

Pure nsC3N4, Bi2WO6, and NGQDs were prepared according to the procedures described in published studies [28–30] with some modifications. The preparation details are described in the Supporting Information (SI).

The composite NGQD/nsC3N4 was fabricated via a simple polymerization technique, in which 3 mg of the prepared NGQDs was dissolved in 1.5 mL of deionized water. The solution was then slowly dropped into 10 g of urea under sonication to facilitate dispersion. The mixture was dehydrated at 60 °C before subsequent annealing to 530 °C for 3 h at a rate of 8 °C•min−1 in a furnace. The obtained yellow–brown product (~300 mg) was then chilled and crushed into a fine powder, which was annealed at 500 °C for 5 h to obtain the final exfoliated white-brown nanosheet NGQD/nsC3N4.

The nsC3N4/Bi2WO6 composite was prepared via a hydrothermal method. First, 330 mg of Na2WO4·2H2O and different amounts of nsC3N4 (150, 400, 850, and 2400 mg) were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 2 h. Subsequently, 972 mg of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of glacial acetic acid and slowly dropped into the suspension solution under vigorous stirring. Next, 200 mL of the mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and maintained in an oven at 160 °C for 16 h. The resultant precipitate was filtered and washed with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, before drying at 60 °C. The resultant composites were denoted as CB20, CB40, CB60, and CB80, representing 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80% (by weight) nsC3N4 in the final product, respectively. Among the hosts and fabricated composites, CB40 exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance (details provided in Section 3). Thus, NCB-0, the ternary composite of NGQD/nsC3N4/Bi2WO6, was synthesized following a similar procedure utilized for CB40, except NGQD/nsC3N4 was utilized instead of nsC3N4. Subsequently, increased amounts of NGQDs (1%, 2%, and 3% of the final product weight) were added to the Na2WO4 precursor solution to improve the charge transport features in Bi2WO6 and at the host interfaces. The obtained products were denoted as NCB-1, NCB-2, and NCB-3 according to the percent weight of NGQDs in the final NCB product. A schematic of the process utilized to synthesize the ternary composites is presented in Fig. S1, and the NGQD dose optimization in the preparation of NCB is detailed in Section 3.

The crystalline structures of the tested materials were investigated via X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max-2500, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA in the 2θ range of 10–70°. The Raman spectra were measured using a high-resolution Raman microscope (Invia, Renishaw) with a He-Ne laser (632.8 nm wavelength). The microscopic morphology of the materials was imaged by a scanning electron microscope (JSM-7500F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer. The microstructure was captured by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F). The particle size distribution and nsC3N4 thickness were determined using a Beckman Counter Delsa Nano analyzer and an atomic force microscope (NanoFocus), respectively. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained with a JASCO-V750 UV–VIS spectrophotometer, and a fluorescence spectrometer (FP-8000, Jasco) was utilized to record the PL spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed using a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with Al Kα x-rays (1486.6 eV), and an IviumStatTM electrochemical analyzer (Model 2011C00026) was employed to measure the photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra.

A solar simulator (SciSun-LP300, Sciencetech) equipped with a 300 W xenon lamp and a 400 nm UV cutoff filter was used as the visible light radiation source. In each trial, 0.25 L of EfOM solution was transferred to a jacketed double-layer flask reactor and maintained at 20 °C in a recirculating cooler. A mass (0.1 g) of the photocatalyst was added to the solution, and a magnetic mixer stirred the suspension under dark conditions for 1 h to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibria between the EfOM and the catalysts (see Fig. S2 for preliminary adsorption time results). At predetermined time intervals over a 6 h period during the photocatalytic reaction, 5 mL aliquots of the slurry were sampled and centrifuged. The supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further analysis, and the catalyst pellet was washed for recycling assessment. The photocatalyst exhibiting the highest performance was selected and tested for EfOM treatment to determine the optimal dose. Different methods were applied to identify the active species occurring in the photocatalytic process. Scavenging experiments were separately conducted using KI, p-benzoquinone, IPA, or furfuryl alcohol as specific quenchers for h+, O2 −, OH, and 1O2, respectively [31–33]. O2 − and 1O2 generation were confirmed through a color reaction with the XTT reagent and chemiluminescence reaction with the SOSG probe, respectively [34]. The radical formation was also monitored via electron spin resonance (ESR, JES-X330, JEOL) using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin-trapping agent for O2 − and OH and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine for trapping 1O2. Additional details are provided in the SI

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical methods for EfOM and surface water DOM characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The DOC concentrations of the EfOM and surface water DOM samples were determined utilizing a TOC analyzer (TOC-L series, Shimadzu), and the fluorescence EEM spectra were recorded using a spectrofluorometer (F-7000, Hitachi). The scanning region covered excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelengths in the range of 250–550 and 280–550 nm with 5 and 1 nm interval steps, respectively. The slit widths for Ex and Em were adjusted to 10 nm, and the photomultiplier voltage was set at 600 V. A 290 nm cutoff filter was installed to eliminate second-order Rayleigh scattering. All samples were adjusted to a pH of ~ 6.7 before optical measurement. To prevent the self-quenching effect (or inner-filter effect), the samples were diluted until the UV absorption coefficient at 254 nm was lower than 0.05 cm−1 [35]. PARAFAC was performed to decompose the overlapping fluorescence peaks in the EEM dataset (210 EEMs) into several independent components using the MATLAB drEEM toolbox (version 2.0). This procedure was previously described by Murphy et al. [36]. The relative abundance of each identified fluorescent component was expressed using the maximum fluorescence intensities of the fluorescent components, which were standardized and normalized to a unified Raman unit considering the water Raman peak area at an Ex wavelength of 350 nm.

Changes in the distributions of the different-sized fractions in the EfOM and surface water DOM samples during photocatalytic degradation were analyzed by SEC-OCD. The system configuration is described by Huber et al. [26]. In this system, a size exclusion column (Toyopearl HW-50S, Tosoh Corp.) was employed to separate different size fractions. Phosphate buffer (pH ~ 6.9) was used as the mobile phase, which was eluted at a flow rate of 1.1 mL•min−1 with a Knauer S-100 pump. To prevent column contamination, the loading samples were diluted to obtain a DOC concentration<5 mgC•L−1. The software installed in the system provided quantitative estimations for five different size fractions from the bulk samples, which include biopolymer (BP, ≥20 kDa), HS (~1 kDa), building blocks (BBs, 0.3–0.5 kDa), low-molecular-weight neutrals (LMWNs, <0.35 kDa), and low-molecular-weight acids (LMWAs, <0.350 kDa).

The XRD patterns of the hosts and composites are presented in Fig. 1 a. Five diffraction peaks were observed for Bi2WO6 at 28.1°, 33°, 47.4°, 56.2°, and 58.8° with no detectable impurities, which corresponded to those of the typical Bi2WO6 orthorhombic phase (JCPDS 39–0256) [37]. One peak occurred in the nsC3N4 spectrum at 27.8° for the (002) facet, which can be assigned to the interlayer stacking patterns of the conjugated π-system. The lack of a peak around 13° for the (100) diffraction plane confirmed the successful exfoliation of the bulk g-C3N4 into nanosheet layers [38]. For the binary composites of nsC3N4/Bi2WO6, the overlapping peaks of nsC3N4 and Bi2WO6 appeared at approximately 28°, and the intensities increased with increased C3N4 amounts in the composite. The XRD pattern of the ternary composite NCB-1 was identical to that of CB40, with no peak reflecting the presence of NGQDs. This may be due to the low NGQD content in NCB-1 in its synthesis. The presence of NGQDs was revealed in the Raman spectrum of NCB-1 (Fig. 1b), which exhibited peaks at 1350 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the carbon D- and G-bands of NGQDs, respectively, along with several bands in the range of 200–900 cm−1 that reflect the O–W vibration of Bi2WO6 [39].

Compared to Bi2WO6, CB40 exhibited a pronounced absorption edge (approximately 450 nm) in the UV-diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 1c), which might be due to the deposition of nsC3N4 on the surface of Bi2WO6. There was a distinct red shift in the NGQD/nsC3N4 absorption edge compared to that of nsC3N4 (495 and 460 nm, respectively), which indicated an improvement in light absorbance, attributed to the incorporation of the photosensitizer NGQDs into nsC3N4. It was also observed that NCB-1 could extend the absorption edge up to 480 nm, increasing the light-harvesting ability in the long-wavelength range (400–800 nm) compared to that of CB40. This is attributed to the specific upconversion PL feature of NGQDs due to their multiphoton active processes [19,20]. As illustrated by the PL spectra of NGQDs (Fig. S3), when the NGQDs were excited by near-infrared light (700–800 nm), shorter-wavelength radiation was emitted within the 400–600 nm range, which could be absorbed by other components in the composite. The optical band gap values were estimated by the Kubelka–Munk function and a Tauc plot of (
α h v
)1/2 against the photon energy,
hv
(Fig. 1d; the applied equation of the Kubelka–Munk method is provided in the SI). By applying these estimating methods, bandgap energies of 2.78 and 2.84 eV were determined for nsC3N4 and Bi2WO6, respectively, which concurs with those determined in previous studies [40,41]. NGQD/nsC3N4 and NCB-1 exhibited distinct bandgap reductions to 2.65 and 2.67 eV, respectively, due to the presence of NGQDs in the composites, which may arise from the supplemental electronic state above the nsC3N4 valence band (VB) [14]. These results suggest that the ternary composite, NCB-1, could efficiently harvest visible light to induce electron-hole separation, enabling photocatalysis.

The transfer and recombination behaviors of the photoinduced charge carriers in the tested materials were assessed using the PL spectra at 380 nm (Ex) (Fig. 1e). The most pronounced peak was displayed by nsC3N4 at approximately 460 nm, attributed to the rapid recombination of excited electrons and holes. The CB40 peak showed a much lower intensity than that of nsC3N4, indicating that the charge recombination rate would be more easily controlled on CB40 because the interlaced structures of the [Bi2O2]+2 and [WO4]−2 layers promote the internal static electric field and enhance charge migration in the heterogeneous composite [42,43]. Among the samples, NCB-1 exhibited the lowest luminescent characteristic peak intensity. Based on these results, NGQD incorporation into the composite appears to accelerate exciton separation and effectively lower the recombination rate.

NCB-1 also exhibited steady, reversible photocurrent versus time curves and the highest current density of all the tested materials throughout multiple intermittent illumination cycles (Fig. 1f). Possessing the highest photocurrent response suggests that NCB-1 efficiently separates photoinduced e−–h+ pairs and enables prompt interfacial charge transmission in the excited NCB-1 composite. The NGQDs play a significant role in facilitating charge transfer due to their presence in the structural network of NCB-1. Zhu et al. [44] reported that the co-existence of p- and n-type domains in NGQDs could significantly improve charge transmission across the NGQD interface through an internal Z-scheme process, thereby increasing the charge transport rate and impeding e−–h+ recombination. The electrochemical impedance spectra acquired for obtaining the electrochemical properties were consistent with the photocurrent measurement results. The semicircles in the Nyquist plot of the electrochemical impedance spectra illustrate the charge transfer kinetics from the operative electrode-solution interaction. A smaller arc radius of the Nyquist semicircle reflects a lower charge transfer resistance [45]. As presented in Fig. 1g, the capacitive circle radii of the hosts and composites were in the order of NCB-1 < CB40 < Bi2WO6 < nsC3N4, which confirmed that the fastest interfacial charge transmission and most effective separation of photoinduced e−–h+ pairs were achieved using the combination of the hosts with NGQD addition in the ternary composite NCB-1.

The morphologies and microstructures of the materials were examined by comparing their field-emission scanning electron microscopy and HR-TEM images, as shown in Figs. 2 and S4. The formation of a thin nsC3N4 (Fig. 2 a, thickness up to 17 mm) from the thermal exfoliation stage was also confirmed (see Fig. S5 for the atomic force micrograph of nsC3N4). NGQD doping did not alter the nsC3N4 morphology (Fig. 2b). Bi2WO6 was microspherical (Fig. 2c and S4a) with a diameter range of 2 to ~ 10 µm (Fig. S6). For NCB-1, the Bi2WO6 microspheres were enwrapped with the NGQD/C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 2d and S4b). Despite multiple washes and the utilization of high ultrasonic dispersion for the HR-TEM measurements, the microparticles with NGQD/C3N4 nanosheets enwrapped with Bi2WO6 were stably maintained (Fig. 2e). Lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.272 and 0.273 nm were observed from the HR-TEM images obtained with a higher magnification (Fig. 2f–h), which can be assigned to the (002) and (200) facets of Bi2WO6, respectively [46]. No distinct lattice fringe was observed from the outer layer, implying an amorphous nsC3N4 structure [47]. Dark dots were distributed across the microsphere with a diameter range of 5–7 nm and lattice intervals of 0.346 nm, which correspond to NGQD nanoparticles with a basal (002) plane [48]. The energy-dispersive X-ray mapping images with different-colored dots (Fig. S7) revealed a uniform distribution of Bi, W, O, C, and N in NCB-1.

The X-ray photoelectron survey spectra of nsC3N4, NGQD/nsC3N4, Bi2WO6, and NCB-1 showed the chemical states and linking characteristics of the constituent elements on their surfaces (Fig. 3 a and S8), which were consistent with the energy-dispersive spectroscopy profiles (Fig. S7) regarding major chemical composition. In the high-resolution spectrum of Bi2WO6 (Fig. 3b and c), the signals at 159.3 and 164.8 eV were attributed to Bi3+ (4f7/2 and 4f5/2 states, respectively), and the signals at 35.6 and 37.8 eV were attributed to W6+ (4f7/2 and 4f5/2 states, respectively) [49]. Peaks were also observed in the NCB-1 spectrum with a minor shift toward lower binding energy, implying electronic interactions between the host, Bi2WO6, and NGQD/nsC3N4. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of NCB-1 showed three deconvoluted peaks at 529.9, 320.9, and 532 eV (Fig. 3d). The first two peaks are associated with Bi–O and W–O bonds in Bi2WO6, respectively, and the third peak is attributed to the C–O connection in NGQDs [17,50]. The N1s spectra of NCB-1 and NGQD/nsC3N4 were deconvoluted individually into three bands at 398.5, 399.8, and 400.8 eV (Fig. 3e), which correspond to nitrogen atoms in the triazine system, including (C = N–C), graphitic-like N, and C–N–H, respectively [14,51,52]. The C1s spectrum of NCB-1 was characterized by the presence of four peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.9, and 289.4 eV (Fig. 3f), which correspond to the carbon atoms in the connections, including C = C, C–O, N–C = N, and O–C = O, respectively [17,45,51]. The C1s spectrum of nsC3N4 (Fig. S8) was characterized by two peaks, a major peak at 288 eV (N–C = N) and a minor peak at 284.8 eV (C = C). The complete introduction of NGQDs into NGQD/nsC3N4 and the ternary composite NCB-1 was confirmed by the intense peaks representing C–N–H and C = C, and the existence of C–O and O–C = O signals in the NGQD/nsC3N4 and NCB-1 spectra.

NCB-0, in which NGQD/nsC3N4 replaced nsC3N4, showed a pronounced DOC removal efficiency of 37.4 ± 0.6%. Furthermore, the addition of 1% NGQDs to CB40 in NCB-1 resulted in an increased DOC removal of 42.3 ± 1.2% (Fig. S9). There was no significant difference in DOC adsorption between CB40 and NCB-1; however, the photocatalytic degradation improved with removal rate improvements from 19.3 ± 1.1% to 27.3 ± 1.5% and the degradation constant, k, from 0.045 to 0.062 h−1, respectively. The degradation enhancement is attributed to NGQD incorporation into the nsC3N4 structure, which widened the range of light absorption via its upconversion PL property. As previously reported [19], NGQD acts as an active site to deliver electrons to the photocatalyst surface and enhance active species generation for the reaction. However, increased NGQD dosages of 2% and 3% did not increase enhancement, but resulted in a TOC removal reduction (Fig. S9). This may be ascribed to the so-called “inner-filter” effect because NGQDs probably compete with nsC3N4 for photon absorption [14], thus reducing the e−–h+ pair separation. Details of the photocatalytic mechanism are provided in Section 3.3.

An increased NCB-1 dose from 0.2 to 1.2 g•L−1 increased the EfOM adsorption from 8.1 ± 0.4% to 43.4 ± 1.5% on the catalyst (Fig. S10 and Table S3), and photocatalytic degradation was enhanced for dosages up to 1 g•L−1 due to the increased numbers of active sites on the catalyst surface and active species generation (Fig. 5 ). The total EfOM removal peaked at a dose of 1 g•L−1 with a removal percentage of 82.8 ± 1.6%; however, above this dosage, the removal efficiency was not improved, probably due to the high turbidity of the suspension and the consequent reduction of penetrable light [12]. Alternatively, dosages exceeding 1 g•L−1 could increase photocatalyst agglomeration via the weak interactions between the nsC3N4 cover and the microspheres, affecting contact efficiency with the organic molecules [54]. The removal efficiency was consistently higher for surface water DOM than for EfOM (paired t-test, p < 0.005) (Table S3), which is attributed to the compositional differences between EfOM and surface water DOM. Additionally, in wastewater effluent, the presence of various inorganics such as metal ions, phosphate, nitrate, and sulfate might impede the ultimate surface activity of the photocatalyst by scavenging active species such as h+ and OH [55].

The applicability of visible-light-driven photocatalysis for EfOM or aquatic DOM treatment has been minimally studied [10], and findings (Table S1) have shown that a comparison of the removal efficiencies of various photocatalytic systems would be nearly impossible owing to the wide discrepancies in target DOM sources and operational parameters. Nevertheless, the NCB-1 photocatalyst outperformed the other materials analyzed in this study in terms of efficiency. Furthermore, the highest removal efficiency was achieved using NCB-1 for water/wastewater samples under mild conditions and without the addition of other chemicals.

Based on the results presented in Fig. 6 b, NCB-1 presumably generated the four investigated active species during photocatalysis, and the addition of scavengers inhibited DOC removal in the order of IPA > KI > p-benzoquinone > furfuryl alcohol. Among the active agents, the strong oxidant OH likely played the most significant role. The addition of IPA (OH quencher) reduced the EfOM and surface water DOM removal by ~ 35.1% and ~ 42.3%, respectively, compared to that of the control (without any inhibitor). The O2 − and 1O2 activities were nearly equal and provided greater removal in EfOM than surface water DOM. Thus, it can be inferred that there is a close association between O2 − and 1O2, and that EfOM contains constituents more vulnerable to O2 − and 1O2 than surface water DOM. The O2 − generation by NCB-1 was confirmed using the XTT probe. As shown in Fig. 6c, the peak for formazan at 475 nm increased with illumination time, implying the generation of a certain amount of O2 − followed by a reaction with XTT to produce more formazan. Meanwhile, the NCB-1 solution containing the SOSG probe emitted an increasing green fluorescent band at 525 nm caused by the reaction of SOSG with 1O2 (Fig. 6d), demonstrating 1O2 formation when NCB-1 was activated by visible light. To further verify the generation of radicals by the photocatalyst NCB-1, the ESR spin-trap technique was performed. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, there were no radical-induced ESR signals in the dark; however, signals were induced under visible light illumination for 10 min by the generation of OH and O2 − radicals. The 1:2:2:1 ratio intensity peaks and 14 G hyperfine coupling constants are attributed to the DMPO spin adduct of OH [56]. The recorded intensity peaks with a 1:1:1:1 ratio are attributed to the DMPO–OOH spin adduct resulting from DMPO trapping O2 − [56,57]. Fig. 6f presents the ESR spectra containing triplet representative signals with a 1:1:1 ratio, which corresponded to the TEMPO adduct from the selective reaction between 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine and 1O2 [58]. The ESR spin-trap technique results were consistent with the scavenging experiment results of this study.

To generate the flow diagram of light-excited charge carriers in NCB-1, the relative VB and conduction band (CB) edge levels of nsC3N4 and Bi2WO6 were identified. Based on the previously measured bandgap values and the empirical equation, the CB edge level (ECB) and VB edge level (EVB) values of Bi2WO6 and nsC3N4 were estimated to be 0.43 and −1.17 eV and 3.27 and 1.61 eV, respectively (calculations are provided in the SI). When NCB-1 is illuminated by visible light, the e−–h+ pairs on nsC3N4 and Bi2WO6 are excited, and e− separates from h+ at the VB and moves to the CB. OH, the major active species, is primarily produced by the reaction of h+ with adsorbed water (Eo = 2.73 eV for OH/H2O [59]), while O2 − is generated from O2 reduction by the CB electrons (Eo = –0.33 eV for O2/·O2 −, [60]). Because only the ECB of nsC3N4 was lower than the standard redox potential of O2/O2 −, and the EVB of Bi2WO6 was higher than the Eo of OH/H2O, O2 − and OH are probably produced at the nsC3N4 CB and Bi2WO6 VB, respectively. Furthermore, because the distance between the Bi2WO6 CB and the nsC3N4 VB is much closer than the bandgap distance between Bi2WO6 and nsC3N4, rapid combination likely occurs among the electrons in the Bi2WO6 CB and the holes in the nsC3N4 VB via enjoined electric fields through interfacial host contact. This charge carrier transfer pathway corresponds to the Z-scheme mechanism, which has been utilized for many other photocatalytic composites [9,37,51]. This mechanism explains the effective charge carrier separation and inhibition of their recombination on the composite, promoting active species generation and improving the photocatalytic activity compared to single-component materials. Moreover, NGQD loading on the composite plays a role in light conversion due to its upconversion PL property and serves as a charge collector, which can improve charge transmission across the NGQD interface through an internal Z-scheme mechanism [44]. NGQDs, with a strong electron affinity, can rapidly deliver the remaining e− onto the nsC3N4 CB to reduce O2 to O2 − [19]. The probable 1O2 production mechanism is via the oxidation of O2 − by trapped holes [61] and OH [62,63], as shown in Eqs. (1)–(3):
(1)
·O2 − + h+ → 1O2
(2)
·O2 − + ·OH → 1O2 + OH−
(3) 2 ·O2 − + 2H2O → 1O2 + H2O2 + 2OH−.
This mechanism highlights the important role of h+ as the OH and 1O2 source in the photocatalytic process of NCB-1; a diagram of this mechanism is presented in Fig. 6a.

Stability tests are required for the practical application of a photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. S11a, the EfOM removal rate of NCB-1 was only reduced by 6.4% after five consecutive cycles (82.8% and 76.4% after the first and fifth cycle, respectively). The slight reduction in performance is attributed to the loss of NCB-1 during the multiple washing steps and centrifugation. The XRD patterns of NCB-1 after five cycles revealed no significant alteration from the original features, confirming its structural stability (Fig. S11b). The field-emission scanning electron micrographs and energy-dispersive X-ray spectra of NCB-1 after the fifth cycle showed no significant alterations in the morphology or elemental constituents (Fig. S12). These results indicate that NCB-1 is structurally stable and durable, and can be recycled several times in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preferential removal with respect to different fluorescent components and size fractions
Removal behaviors of different fluorescent components
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, five independent fluorescent components were identified from 210 EEM datasets by PARAFAC modeling with an explained variation and core consistency of 95.7% and 93.3%, respectively. The contour plots of the identified components are presented in Fig. S13. The five components were well matched with those reported in previous studies, with a similarity > 95% in the OpenFluor online library [64]. Component 1 (C1) possessed maxima at 250/480 and ~ 340/480 nm (Ex/Em), attributed to humic-like components with terrestrial origins that can be characterized by a large molecular size and the enrichment of condensed aromatic structures [65,66]. Component 2 (C2) exhibited peaks at < 250/450 and 325/450 nm (Ex/Em), which were consistent with the A and C peaks of typical humic-like fluorescence in marine or riverine environments [67,68]. Yamashita et al. observed a similar humic-like fluorophore in wastewater effluent but with a lower intensity [69]. Mostofa et al. also reported a similar peak in samples collected from domestic sewage and assigned it to a household detergent-like component [70]. Component 3 (C3), which exhibited peaks at < 250/410 and 320/410 nm (Ex/Em), has been reported as a refractory humic-like component in wastewater after biological treatment [71,72]. Component 4 (C4) showed a prominent peak at 280/400 nm (Ex/Em), which corresponds to that of microbial humic-like fluorescence in surface water DOM samples [23,73]. C4 has also been mentioned as a fluorescent component generated from biological wastewater treatment processes [22,74]. In this study, C4 was assigned as a low-molecular-weight humic-like component due to its peak at shorter Em wavelengths than that of C2 [9,21,53,75]. These humic-like fluorescent components, which have higher aromatic contents and larger molecular sizes, are ranked in the order of C4 < C3 < C2 < C1. The only non humic-like fluorescent component, Component 5 (C5), exhibited a peak at 275/325 nm (Ex/Em), which is related to protein/amino acid-like substances [11,69]. The relative distributions of the five components are listed in Table S4.

With the addition of NCB-1, the total removal rates of all the fluorescent components except C4 remained high after 6 h of illumination (Fig. 7 and Table S5) and ranged from 91.5 ± 2.2% to 99.3 ± 0.3%. The humic-like components had relatively high adsorption capacities in the order of C2 > C3 > C4 for EfOM and C1 > C2 > C4 for surface water DOM. The adsorption preference was consistent with the relative molecular sizes and the enrichment of condensed aromatic structures among the humic-like components. Therefore, adsorption seems to be driven mainly by π-π and hydrophobic interactions between organic molecules and the π-conjugated surface of NCB-1. This is supported by the fact that the structures of both nsC3N4 and NGQDs possess a π‐conjugated system, which can be favorable for the adsorption of aromatic structures [9,53,76].

All the fluorescent components showed a higher degradation constant (k) than that of bulk DOC, attributed to the rapid decay of aromatic chromophores into the non-light-absorbing organic byproducts under photocatalysis. Degradation rate differences were observed among the fluorescent components with a higher k value for C2 than for C3 in EfOM (0.543 ± 0.026 and 0.333 ± 0.021 h−1, respectively) and for C1 than for C2 in surface water DOM (0.604 ± 0.006 and 0.429 ± 0.011 h−1, respectively). These differences can be ascribed to the preferential attack of major active species on compounds enriched with unsaturated bonds and functional groups. It was previously reported that the presence of electron-donor functional groups considerably increased aromatic degradation by enhancing reactivity and diminishing the activation energy barrier [77,78]. Because 1O2 and O2 − are mild oxidants of condensed aromatics, OH could be the major component in the degradation of humic-like components [79]. The low degradation rate of C3 suggests its recalcitrant property against oxidation [80]. Conversely, the higher degradation rate of C5 relative to the humic-like components can be attributed to the synergetic effects of all the active oxygen species. In addition to OH, 1O2 reportedly participates in the breakdown of proteinaceous substances through multiple reactions [81]. 1O2 might rapidly decompose proteins possessing electron-donating double bonds activated by alkoxy and amino functional groups, producing peroxy radicals and peroxides [82]. The high degradation rate of C5 also implies that it might be derived from low-molecular-weight protein-like fluorophores such as tryptophan [23,83]. The calculated high rate constants of tryptophan and tryptophanamide with 1O2 were approximately 3.3 × 107 and 2.6 × 107 M−1s−1, respectively [84]. In contrast to the other components, C4 exhibited a negative degradation kinetic rate during the early phase of oxidation, followed by wide variations throughout the photocatalytic process (Fig. 7). This result indicates that the byproducts from the photocatalytic breakdown of larger molecules associated with C1 or C2 possibly contain the low-molecular-weight humic-like component C4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal behaviors of different size fractions within bulk DOM
ce:section.ce:para: Five different size fractions were identified from the SEC-OCD chromatograms, and the removal behaviors were tracked for the individual fractions. The high BB (14.6%), LMWA (8.7%), and LMWN (17.9%) abundances in the original EfOM sample (Fig. S15) agreed with the findings of a previous study [85]. A substantial reduction in large size fractions was observed after adsorption onto NCB-1 (Fig. S16b), which was consistent with the observation of the large-molecular-sized C2 in this study. Additionally, the BP fraction significantly decreased from 1.03 to 0.64 mgC L−1 after 1 h of illumination (Table S6), which may be attributed to the synergetic effects of the active oxidizing agents 1O2 and OH in removing low-molecular-weight unsaturated BP. However, the degradation progressed slowly after the first hour with an additional removal of only 20.1% in the subsequent 5 h of irradiation, and 34.1% of the BP remained, suggesting a recalcitrant property of BP against photocatalytic decomposition [86]. After illumination for 2 h (Fig. S16d), relatively smaller size fractions (e.g., BB, LMWA, and LMWN) became more abundant, probably due to the breakdown of the larger size fractions such as HS and BP [27]. The removal increased between 2 and 4 h of irradiation in the order of BB > LMWA > LMWN (Fig. S16e), indicating the production of the smaller-sized fractions, LMWA and LMWN, at the expense of BB. A similar pattern was also observed for DOM in the surface water samples (Fig. S17).

The size distribution changes correspond with those in previous photocatalysis studies. For instance, Espinoza et al. demonstrated the preferential adsorption and decomposition of a bog lake high-molecular-weight humic material via UV/TiO2 oxidation, followed by the formation of smaller byproducts containing LMWA [27]. Valencia et al. also described a similar gradual molecular size-reduction trend and an increase in unsaturated electrophilic structures in aquatic DOM via photocatalysis based on the UV/TiO2 p25 process [87].

The specific responses and alterations of different EfOM components and size fractions upon photocatalysis can reveal the underlying photocatalytic mechanisms and help identify the environmental consequences of treatment processes. For example, the effective removal of the high-molecular-weight humic-like components C1 and C2 imparts an additional benefit of reduced disinfection byproduct formation in EfOM treated by the photocatalyst NCB-1, because HS are highly correlated with the precursor materials of disinfection byproducts [88]. In contrast, the high degradation rate of the protein-like component C5 indicates that the utilization of NCB-1 can effectively mitigate membrane fouling in membrane filtration post-treatment processes and lower the risk of microbial regrowth in water distribution systems. Considering the residual low-molecular-weight fractions and recalcitrant compounds in the treated EfOM, a post-treatment option based on activated carbon adsorption is recommended [89,90].

In this study, the visible light-activated photocatalyst NCB-1, synthesized from NGQD, nsC3N4, and Bi2WO6, exhibited excellent removal efficiency toward EfOM after multiple cycles, and the structural, morphological, and optical properties of the materials elucidated the photocatalytic mechanisms. The combination of nsC3N4 and Bi2WO6 generated multiple active species for EfOM degradation via effective Z-scheme charge transfer pathways. The introduction of NGQDs, with upconversion PL and tunable electronic properties, into the structural network, was also determined as a fundamental factor for improving the photocatalytic activity of NCB-1. EEM-PARAFAC and SEC-OCD analyses provided further insights into the complex removal mechanism of EfOM by determining the individual removal behaviors of different fluorophores and size fractions. Higher adsorbabilities were observed for the components possessing relatively larger molecular sizes and aromatic structures due to the formation of π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions. OH probably plays an important role in degrading the humic-like fluorescent components, while the synergetic effects of OH and 1O2 assist in breaking down the proteinaceous fluorophores. Rapid HS decomposition via photocatalysis resulted in the production of smaller molecules, while increased LMWN amounts resulted from the rapid destruction of BB and a portion of the BP fraction. This study demonstrated an effective, economical, visible light-activated photocatalyst with a high EfOM removal capability for wastewater effluent reclamation.
